items.title: Monitor validation, not training loss
items.avoid: EarlyStopping(monitor="loss") or stopping when training loss plateaus.
items.prefer: Monitor val/loss, val/f1, or val/rmse with mode matching the metric (min for loss, max for F1).
items.why: Training loss can improve while validation worsens — the overfitting signal lives on the validation curve.

items.title: Always restore best weights at stop
items.avoid: Using the model state at the epoch when patience expired.
items.prefer: Reload best checkpoint — restore_best_weights, saved state_dict, or best_iteration for prediction.
items.why: Stop epoch is typically several passes after peak validation — final weights are often the worst generalization in the late tail.
items.example: PyTorch: model.load_state_dict(best_state) after the training loop, not model.state_dict() from the last epoch.

items.title: Tune patience for validation noise
items.avoid: Copying patience=3 from a tutorial when your validation set has 200 rows and noisy metrics.
items.prefer: Start with patience 10–20 for small val sets; add min_delta to ignore insignificant improvements.
items.why: Patience is a variance–bias trade-off — too low stops on noise; too high trains into overfitting.
items.example: 5000-row val set: patience=5 may suffice. 200-row val set: patience=15 with min_delta=0.001 on val_loss.

items.title: Log train and val curves together
items.avoid: Logging only validation metrics without the training series for context.
items.prefer: Plot train/loss and val/loss on the same chart each run — divergence confirms early stopping is working.
items.why: Side-by-side curves reveal underfitting (both flat), healthy learning (both fall), and overfitting (train falls, val rises).
items.example: W&B log: train/loss and val/loss vs epoch — annotate vertical line at stop epoch and marker at best epoch.
